---
title: "Lesson 6: Variance and Covariance"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_variance-and-covariance.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_variance-and-covariance.en.txt){target="_blank"}
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-variance-and-covariance_module1_lesson5.pdf){width=700 height=700}
:::

## Variance {#sec-variance}

::: {#def-variance}
## Variance

Let $X$ be a random variable with mean

$$
\mu_X = \mathbb{E}[X].
$$

The variance of $X$ is

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(X-\mu_X)^2\right].
$$

Variance is also denoted by $\sigma_X^2$, or simply $\sigma^2$ when the random variable is clear from context.
:::

Variance measures the spread of a distribution around its mean.

The unsquared deviation does not provide a measure of spread because

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X-\mu_X]
&= \mathbb{E}[X]-\mu_X \\
&= \mu_X-\mu_X \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Positive and negative deviations cancel. The mean absolute deviation

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[|X-\mu_X|\right]
$$

is a valid measure of spread, but squared deviations are generally more convenient algebraically.

## Standard Deviation {#sec-standard-deviation}

::: {#def-standard-deviation}
## Standard deviation

The standard deviation of $X$ is the nonnegative square root of its variance:

$$
\operatorname{SD}(X)
= \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}
= \sqrt{\sigma_X^2}
= \sigma_X.
$$
:::

Unlike variance, standard deviation has the same physical units as $X$.

For example, if

$$
X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

then $\mu$ is the center of the distribution and $\sigma$ measures its spread. The points $\mu-\sigma$ and $\mu+\sigma$ lie one standard deviation from the mean.

## An Alternative Formula for Variance {#sec-variance-alternative}

::: {#prp-variance-computational}
## Computational formula for variance

For any random variable $X$ with finite second moment,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - \left(\mathbb{E}[X]\right)^2.
$$
:::

::: {#prf-variance-computational .proof}
## Derivation

Let $\mu_X=\mathbb{E}[X]$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(X)
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(X-\mu_X)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[X^2-2\mu_X X+\mu_X^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] -2\mu_X\mathbb{E}[X] +\mu_X^2 \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] -2\mu_X^2 +\mu_X^2 \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] -\mu_X^2 \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] -\left(\mathbb{E}[X]\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## Scaling a Random Variable {#sec-variance-scaling}

::: {#prp-variance-scaling}
## Variance under scaling

For any constant $a$,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(aX) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(X).
$$
:::

::: {#prf-variance-scaling .proof}
## Derivation

Let $Y=aX$ its mean is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_Y
&= \mathbb{E}[Y] \\ 
&= \mathbb{E}[aX] \\
&= a\mathbb{E}[X] \\
&= a\mu_X.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(aX)
&= \operatorname{Var}(Y) \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(Y-\mu_Y)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(aX-a\mu_X)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[a^2(X-\mu_X)^2\right] \\
&= a^2\mathbb{E}\!\left[(X-\mu_X)^2\right] \\
&= a^2\operatorname{Var}(X).
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## Why Variances Do Not Always Add {#sec-variance-sum-question}

In general,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) \neq \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y).
$$

The missing term describes how $X$ and $Y$ vary together. This leads to covariance.

## Covariance {#sec-covariance}

::: {#def-covariance}
## Covariance

Let $X$ and $Y$ be random variables with means

$$
\mu_X=\mathbb{E}[X],
\qquad
\mu_Y=\mathbb{E}[Y].
$$

Their covariance is

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ (X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y) \right].
$$

Covariance is also denoted by $\sigma_{X,Y}$.
:::

Variance is a special case of covariance:

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,X) = \operatorname{Var}(X).
$$

## Interpreting Covariance {#sec-covariance-interpretation}

The product

$$
(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)
$$

is positive when $X$ and $Y$ lie on the same side of their respective means, and negative when they lie on opposite sides.

::: {#exm-positive-covariance}
## Positive covariance

A scatterplot with an upward trend has positive covariance. Most observations satisfy either

$$
X-\mu_X>0 \quad\text{and}\quad  Y-\mu_Y>0
$$

or

$$
X-\mu_X<0 \quad\text{and}\quad  Y-\mu_Y<0
$$

In both cases,
$(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)>0$
:::

::: {#exm-negative-covariance}
## Negative covariance

A scatterplot with a downward trend has negative covariance. Large values of $X$ tend to occur with small values of $Y$, and small values of $X$ tend to occur with large values of $Y$. Thus the centered deviations generally have opposite signs.
:::

::: {#exm-zero-covariance}
## Covariance close to zero

When a scatterplot has no clear linear upward or downward tendency, positive and negative products of centered deviations tend to cancel, producing covariance close to zero.
:::

## Covariance Depends on Scale {#sec-covariance-scale}

Covariance has no fixed upper or lower bound, and its units are the product of the units of $X$ and $Y$.

Rescaling the variables changes the magnitude of covariance:

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(aX,bY) = ab\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)
$$

Consequently, two scatterplots can show essentially the same linear pattern but have different covariance magnitudes merely because their variables are measured on different scales.

## Correlation {#sec-correlation}

::: {#def-correlation}
## Correlation

For random variables with positive finite variances, their correlation is

$$
\operatorname{Corr}(X,Y) := \frac{ \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) }{ \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)\operatorname{Var}(Y)} }.
$$

Equivalently,

$$
\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\sigma_{X,Y}}{\sigma_X\sigma_Y}
$$
:::

Correlation is dimensionless and satisfies
$-1 \leq \rho_{X,Y} \leq 1$

It retains the sign of covariance while removing its dependence on measurement units.

## An Alternative Formula for Covariance {#sec-covariance-alternative}

::: {#prp-covariance-computational}
## Computational formula for covariance

For random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite second moments,

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]
$$
:::

::: {#prf-covariance-computational .proof}
## Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ (X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y) \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ XY-\mu_YX-\mu_XY+\mu_X\mu_Y \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[XY] -\mu_Y\mathbb{E}[X] -\mu_X\mathbb{E}[Y] +\mu_X\mu_Y \\
&= \mathbb{E}[XY] -\mu_X\mu_Y \\
&= \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## Independence and Zero Covariance {#sec-independence-zero-covariance}

::: {#prp-independence-uncorrelated}
## Independence implies zero covariance

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent and have finite second moments, then

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0.
$$
:::

Independence gives

$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]
$$

so the alternative covariance formula yields zero.

The converse is false: zero covariance does not generally imply independence.

Moreover, when $\operatorname{Var}(X)>0$ and $\operatorname{Var}(Y)>0$,

$$
\operatorname{Corr}(X,Y)=0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0.
$$

Random variables with zero covariance are called **uncorrelated**.

## Example: Uncorrelated but Dependent {#sec-uncorrelated-dependent-example}

::: {#exm-uncorrelated-dependent}
## A nonlinear dependence

Let $X$ have distribution

| $x$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $P(X=x)$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac12$ | $\frac14$ |

Define

$$
Y=X^2.
$$

First,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] 
&= (-1)\left(\frac14\right) + 0\left(\frac12\right) + 1\left(\frac14\right) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Also,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[XY]
&= \mathbb{E}[X^3] \\
&= (-1)^3\left(\frac14\right) + 0^3\left(\frac12\right) + 1^3\left(\frac14\right) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)
&= \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] \\
&= 0-0\cdot\mathbb{E}[Y] \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Nevertheless, $X$ and $Y$ are not independent because $Y$ is determined by $X$. For example,

$$
P(Y=0\mid X=0)=1
$$

whereas
$P(Y=0)=\frac12$
Thus,
$$
\text{independence} \implies \text{uncorrelated}
$$

but

$$
\text{uncorrelated} \nRightarrow \text{independence}.
$$
:::

## Variance of a Sum {#sec-variance-of-sum}

::: {#prp-variance-sum}
## Variance of two-variable sum

For random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite second moments,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) + \operatorname{Var}(Y).
$$
:::

::: {#prf-variance-sum .proof}
## Derivation

Let
$Z=X+Y$
Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_Z
&= \mathbb{E}[Z] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X+Y] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X]+ \mathbb{E}[Y] \\
&= \mu_X+\mu_Y.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y)
&= \operatorname{Var}(Z) \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(Z-\mu_Z)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \left((X+Y)-(\mu_X+\mu_Y)\right)^2 \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \left((X-\mu_X)+(Y-\mu_Y)\right)^2 \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(X-\mu_X)^2\right] \\
&\quad + 2\mathbb{E}\!\left[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)\right] \\
&\quad + \mathbb{E}\!\left[(Y-\mu_Y)^2\right] \\
&= \operatorname{Var}(X) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) + \operatorname{Var}(Y).
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0$, so

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y).
$$


## Reflection
